_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Oglethorpe County, GA. Record survival, courthouse history. Oglethorpe County, formed 1793, has records from near its formation. The courthouse at Lexington survived the Civil War.

previous_courthouses.fate: Early courthouses replaced. No major fire or war damage to records.

record_losses.cause: No major fire or war damage
record_losses.description: Oglethorpe County's records survive from near its 1793 formation with good continuity. The courthouse at Lexington was not destroyed during the Civil War despite Sherman's March passing through the county.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Oglethorpe County records run from the 1790s — if {ancestor_name} was in the county, the paper trail extends to the earliest period of Georgia Piedmont plantation settlement.
record_losses.source: GA Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records.

deeds.repository: Oglethorpe County Clerk of Superior Court; GA Archives microfilm

wills_estates.gaps: No major gaps. Antebellum estate inventories detail enslaved persons.
wills_estates.repository: Oglethorpe County Probate Court; GA Archives

court_minutes.repository: Oglethorpe County Clerk of Superior Court; GA Archives

substitute_sources.description: Oglethorpe County was settled under the headright system. Early grants document the first European settlers and their household sizes.
substitute_sources.repository: GA Department of Archives and History
substitute_sources.source: Cadle, Georgia Land Surveying History and Law (1991).

substitute_sources.source_type: Federal census schedules and slave schedules
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential. Oglethorpe County's high slaveholding makes the 1850-1860 slave schedules particularly important.

substitute_sources.description: The Freedmen's Bureau operated in Oglethorpe County during Reconstruction, documenting labor contracts, school reports, and complaints from freedpeople.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives; FamilySearch digitization of Freedmen's Bureau records
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for tracing newly freed families. Labor contracts list freedpeople by name and their former enslavers.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives, Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands.

narrative_hooks: Oglethorpe County's records go back to the 1790s — if {ancestor_name} was there, the deed chain may extend to the county's earliest settlement, before the cotton gin revolution transformed the landscape.

If {ancestor_name} was enslaved in Oglethorpe County, the planter's estate inventory at the county probate court may list {pronoun_object} by name — and the Freedmen's Bureau records may document the transition to freedom.
Georgia vital registration began in 1919 — the absence of a birth record for {ancestor_name} before that date is standard for the entire state.
Oglethorpe County was settled under the headright system — {ancestor_name}'s original land grant was a petition-based process that documented family size and sometimes origin.
source_references: GA Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records.
Cadle, Farris W. Georgia Land Surveying History and Law. University of Georgia Press, 1991.
National Archives. Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands.
Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: Georgia. Atlanta, 1937-1942.
